title: Chapter 1: The Hunger That Woke Him
content: The pain came first. Then the hunger.

Kael's eyes snapped open to darkness that shouldn't have been visible — but he could count the mortar lines between bricks thirty feet away. Every scratch on every stone. The dust motes hanging in air that smelled of old blood and wet concrete.

He tried to breathe and his lungs didn't cooperate. They'd stopped being lungs three hours ago, though he didn't know that yet. What he knew was the *hunger*. It wasn't in his stomach. It was in his bones. A desperate, animal need that made his hands shake and his mouth fill with something sharp.
Fangs. He had fangs.
*No. No, no—*

The memory came in fragments. The patrol. Third Platoon, Silver Dawn scouts, pushing into the Ruins like they'd done a hundred times before. Kael had been point — he was always point, because he was the one who volunteered for the rotations no one else wanted. Not out of bravery. Out of the quiet certainty that if something went wrong, he'd rather be the first to know.
Something had gone wrong.

The vampires came from everywhere. Not the cautious, territorial skirmishes the Dawn was used to. This was a full assault. Dozens of them — fledglings, Barons, at least one Viscount whose face Kael hadn't seen before the darkness took him. He remembered the impact to his helmet. The cold stone against his cheek. And then teeth.
Someone had turned him. Not killed. *Turned*. And then abandoned him here.

Kael pushed himself upright. The room was a collapsed basement — concrete walls, rusted rebar, a shattered stairway leading up to what might have been daylight. Might have been. He couldn't tell yet because the clock in his head was new and unfamiliar, a pressure behind his eyes that counted down to something terrible.
Dawn. He could feel dawn coming.

He'd studied vampire biology as a scout. Every Silver Dawn recruit did. Fledglings were the lowest rank — cannon fodder, disposable, turned for a single mission and then left to die. Their makers didn't protect them. Didn't teach them. Didn't even name them. They were tools, not progeny, and every vampire in the territory knew it.
*Free blood*, the instructors had called them. *Unclaimed fledglings are fair game. If you find one, stake it. The other vampires will thank you.*
Kael laughed. It came out raw and wrong, his vocal cords not quite working the way they used to. A Silver Dawn scout, turned into vampire bait. The universe had a sense of humor.
The first hour was the worst.

He learned to move before he learned to think. His legs propelled him up the shattered stairway faster than any human sprint. His hands caught the broken railing and he sailed over the gap without conscious decision, landing in a crouch that felt as natural as running had when he'd been alive.
*When he'd been alive.* He was already thinking in past tense.

The Ruins stretched around him. Prague, or what was left of it — thirty years after the Bleeding, the city was a graveyard of concrete and rust. Crashed buses lay on their sides like beached whales. Apartment buildings gaped open, their walls peeled away by decades of neglect. In the distance, the skeletal arms of construction cranes pointed at a grey sky that was just beginning to lighten.

Kael's new senses painted the world in layers. He could hear a woman crying three streets over — human, frightened, her heartbeat a frantic drum. He could smell the iron in her blood from here. He could smell the vampire who was hunting her, too — a rank he couldn't quite identify, something above fledgling but not by much. A Baron, maybe.
His stomach convulsed.
*No.* He clenched his fists. He'd been a hunter. He'd killed vampires. He wasn't going to become one of the things he'd hated, not in the first—
The hunger hit again. Harder. His vision went red at the edges.
He needed blood. Now. From anyone. The instinct was so powerful it physically hurt to resist. His fangs extended fully, aching, demanding use.
Kael forced himself to stop. To breathe — or whatever the vampire equivalent of breathing was. To think.

*You're a scout*, he told himself. *You've survived worse. You've survived the Bleeding, the patrols, the winter of '39 when half the platoon froze to death before the vampires even found them. You can survive this.*
He just had to figure out what "this" was.
The tracking howl came from the south.

It wasn't a wolf. Kael's new ears could tell the difference — wolves were organic, animal, comprehensible. This was something else. A sound designed to carry through vampire senses, to announce: *I am hunting. This territory is claimed. Any unclaimed blood belongs to me.*
Kael ran.

The world blurred. He vaulted a crashed bus without thinking, his body sailing fifteen feet through the air, landing in a roll that would have broken human bones but barely registered on his new nerves. He slipped through a collapsed building, through a courtyard full of rusted vehicles, through streets that twisted and turned in patterns designed to confuse daylight hunters.
The howl came again. Closer.

"I know you're here, little lamb." The voice came from everywhere and nowhere at once — a Baron's trick, projecting sound through the shadows. "Didn't your maker teach you the rules? Unclaimed fledglings don't get to run. They don't get to hide. They get *harvested*."

Kael pressed himself against a wall, forcing his breathing to slow. The hunger was still screaming but fear was louder now. A Baron. He couldn't fight a Baron. He couldn't outrun one, either — Barons had shadow-step, the ability to dissolve and reform anywhere in their territory.
But Barons also had weaknesses. Every vampire did.
*Sunlight*, Kael thought. *The sun kills all of us. All of me now. Dawn is—*
The pressure behind his eyes tightened. The clock was almost at zero.
And he was still in the open.

The Baron's laugh echoed through the Ruins. "Oh, you feel it, don't you? The dawn. The burn. Let me give you some advice, little lamb: find a hole. Find a deep, dark hole and pray the sun doesn't reach it. Because in about ten minutes, the sky is going to catch fire, and you're going to learn exactly what happens to fledglings who don't have a coffin to crawl into."
The voice faded. The Baron was retreating — not out of mercy, but out of self-preservation. Even a Baron couldn't stay in the open at dawn.

Kael looked around desperately. The Ruins offered no shelter. No basement deep enough. No building intact enough. No coffin — he didn't even know where vampires *got* coffins. Did you have to make your own? Buy one? Steal one from a funeral home that had been abandoned since the Bleeding?
The sky was definitely getting lighter.
*Think*, he commanded himself. *You've been hunting vampires for five years. What do they do when they're trapped above ground at dawn?*
They died. That was the answer. They died screaming.

But Kael wasn't ready to die. Not after surviving the turning. Not after whatever had happened to give him these new senses, this new strength, this hunger that wouldn't shut up. He'd been given something — he didn't know if it was a curse or a weapon — and he wasn't going to throw it away by burning in the first sunrise.
He spotted a church.

The Church of Saint Michael's. Half-collapsed, its roof open to the sky, its stained glass shattered into a mosaic of colored knives on the floor. It wouldn't protect him from sunlight — the roof was gone — but it had a crypt. Churches always had crypts.
Kael ran toward it as the first grey light of dawn touched the horizon.
And somewhere in the darkness of the church, a dying woman opened her eyes and heard his footsteps.
